title: Prime editing for precise genome engineering and modulation of fungal metabolism - Nature Biotechnology
gist: University of Pennsylvania researchers engineered fPE7max, a fungi-optimized prime editing platform reaching ~90% average editing efficiency and supporting insertions up to 1 kb and deletions up to 10 kb across multiple filamentous fungal species, then used it to edit upstream open reading frames of the master regulator laeA and unlock 18 metabolites (8 novel, 3 cytotoxic) from previously silent biosynthetic pathways.

insights.claim: fPE7max is a prime editing platform specifically optimized for filamentous fungi, achieving an average editing efficiency approaching 90% across diverse genomic loci and multiple fungal species.
insights.speaker: Sun, Nie, Straub, Keum, Shen & Gao
insights.so_what: A ~90% efficiency prime editor de-risks fungal strain engineering programs for industrial biotech (enzymes, natural products) by making precise edits routine rather than a low-yield bottleneck requiring heavy screening.
insights.quote: we develop fPE7max, a prime editing platform optimized for fungi, which supports different edit types, including base substitutions and defined small insertions or deletions, with an average editing efficiency approaching 90%, across diverse genomic loci and species

insights.claim: fPE7max extends prime editing beyond point mutations and small indels to larger-scale genome edits: insertions of up to 1 kb and deletions of up to 10 kb.
insights.speaker: Sun, Nie, Straub, Keum, Shen & Gao
insights.so_what: 10-kb deletion and 1-kb insertion capability puts prime editing within range of whole-gene-cluster manipulation, a scale previously requiring CRISPR-Cas9 double-strand breaks with associated risk of unwanted indels/translocations.
insights.quote: fPE7max further enables larger insertions of up to 1 kb and deletions of up to 10 kb.

insights.claim: Before fPE7max, prime editing had not been established as a working technique in filamentous fungi at all, despite fungi being both major ecological contributors and industrial hosts with vast biosynthetic capacity.
insights.speaker: Sun, Nie, Straub, Keum, Shen & Gao
insights.so_what: This is a first-mover technology claim: any industrial fungal strain-engineering program (antibiotics, enzymes, biofuels) previously locked out of prime editing's precision now has a validated route in, opening a new tooling market.
insights.quote: Prime editing has not been established in filamentous fungi, which are major ecological contributors and industrial hosts with vast biosynthetic capacity.

insights.claim: pegRNA stability decreases as insert size increases (tested at 50 bp, 100 bp, and 500 bp), meaning larger designed insertions face a built-in efficiency penalty from reduced guide RNA abundance, not just editing machinery limits.
insights.speaker: Sun, Nie, Straub, Keum, Shen & Gao
insights.so_what: Any team scaling insertion size with this platform should budget for pegRNA stabilization engineering as a rate-limiting variable, not just editor enzyme optimization.
insights.quote: Decreased abundance with increasing insert size reflects reduced pegRNA stability for longer templates.

themes.theme: Mismatch repair suppression as an efficiency lever

insights.claim: Suppressing DNA mismatch repair (MMR) genes via shRNA-mediated gene silencing enhances prime editing efficiency, particularly for large edits, when combined with PE3-mediated editing.
insights.speaker: Sun, Nie, Straub, Keum, Shen & Gao
insights.so_what: MMR suppression is a generalizable efficiency lever (already known in mammalian prime editing) that transfers to fungal systems, giving engineers a reusable trick to boost yield on large-edit designs without redesigning the editor itself.
insights.quote: shRNA-mediated gene silencing enhances prime editing efficiency through MMR suppression.

insights.claim: The fungal host's native non-homologous end joining (NHEJ) status, controlled by the nkuA gene, materially affects prime editing outcomes: the authors compared an nkuA-deficient strain (LO8030) against an NHEJ-restored strain (LO8030 argB::nkuA) across six representative single-base and small-indel edits plus a range of large insertions/deletions.
insights.speaker: Sun, Nie, Straub, Keum, Shen & Gao
insights.so_what: Host DNA-repair background is a design variable for prime editing campaigns in new fungal chassis: strain engineers should characterize and potentially knock out nkuA/NHEJ activity before deploying large-edit prime editing to maximize precision outcomes.

insights.claim: fPE7max supports multiplex prime editing (MPE) at three genomic loci simultaneously (wA0 100-bp insertion, chaA +7 C-to-T substitution, cicF 1-kb deletion) using a single polycistronic pegRNA array processed by the endogenous tRNA machinery to release individual functional pegRNAs.
insights.speaker: Sun, Nie, Straub, Keum, Shen & Gao
insights.so_what: Single-transcript multiplexing means combinatorial strain engineering (stacking several edits in one transformation round) becomes practical, sharply cutting the number of engineering cycles needed to build complex production strains.
insights.quote: Schematic of the polycistronic pegRNA array used for simultaneous editing at three loci: wA0 (100-bp insertion), chaA (+7 C to T substitution), and cicF (1-kb deletion).

themes.theme: uORF editing of laeA unlocks silent biosynthesis

insights.claim: The authors used fPE7max to perturb upstream open reading frames (uORFs) in laeA, a pleiotropic (master) regulator gene controlling fungal secondary metabolism, in order to modulate metabolic output across multiple fungal species.
insights.speaker: Sun, Nie, Straub, Keum, Shen & Gao
insights.so_what: Targeting a regulatory uORF rather than the coding sequence is a precision alternative to blunt overexpression, giving tunable control of a master regulator without needing strong constitutive promoters or extra plasmids.
insights.quote: We perturb upstream open reading frames in the pleiotropic regulator gene, laeA, to modulate metabolic output across multiple fungal species.

insights.claim: Editing laeA's uORF via prime editing activated previously lowly-expressed (silent or cryptic) biosynthetic pathways, revealed through metabolomic profiling that identified 18 metabolites, 8 of which were previously unreported structures.
insights.speaker: Sun, Nie, Straub, Keum, Shen & Gao
insights.so_what: This is a genome-mining discovery engine: a single regulatory edit surfaced 8 entirely new chemical structures, suggesting fungal genomes still hold large amounts of untapped chemistry accessible via targeted regulatory editing rather than exhaustive strain screening.
insights.quote: Metabolomic profiling reveals activation of previously lowly biosynthetic pathways, leading to the identification of 18 metabolites, including 8, to our knowledge, previously unreported structures, 3 of which with cytotoxic activity.

insights.claim: Three of the 8 newly discovered metabolite structures showed cytotoxic activity, and the isolates were further tested for antiproliferation activity against four human cancer cell lines: Jurkat, K562, MCF-7, and HepG2.
insights.speaker: Sun, Nie, Straub, Keum, Shen & Gao
insights.so_what: Cytotoxic-active novel structures from a single regulatory-editing screen give a direct pipeline hook for oncology natural-product drug discovery, positioning this platform as a lead-generation tool, not just a genome-engineering method.

insights.claim: Deletion of the laeA uORF in Aspergillus niger activated the pyranonigrin biosynthetic gene cluster (pynA), demonstrably increasing pynA mRNA levels compared to wild-type and a partial-mutant control strain.
insights.speaker: Sun, Nie, Straub, Keum, Shen & Gao
insights.so_what: A concrete causal link (uORF deletion to cluster activation to elevated transcript to elevated pyranonigrin metabolites) validates uORF editing as a reliable dial for de-repressing specific target gene clusters, not just a diffuse metabolic perturbation.
insights.quote: Deletion of the laeA uORF activates the pyranonigrin biosynthetic gene cluster in A. niger.

insights.claim: The laeA uORF-editing strategy generalized across distantly related filamentous fungal species — Trichoderma atroviride, Trichoderma citrinoviride, and Chaetomium globosum — each showing broad activation of clustered biosynthetic pathways upon uORF deletion, evidenced by a predominant rightward shift in molecular-network features on volcano plots.
insights.speaker: Sun, Nie, Straub, Keum, Shen & Gao
insights.so_what: Cross-species generalizability (not just one lab strain) suggests the uORF-editing approach is a portable platform strategy applicable to industrially relevant fungal genera beyond Aspergillus, widening the addressable strain-engineering market.
insights.quote: The predominant rightward shift of colored network members indicates broad activation of clustered biosynthetic pathways in the edited strains.

insights.claim: The paper frames uORF editing broadly as 'a strategy for modulating endogenous regulatory networks and accessing the fungal chemical repertoire,' positioning it as a generalizable regulatory-engineering tool beyond the specific laeA case study.
insights.speaker: Sun, Nie, Straub, Keum, Shen & Gao
insights.so_what: This reframes the target space for natural-product genome mining: instead of activating one gene cluster at a time via promoter swaps, teams can edit upstream regulatory uORFs of master regulators to de-repress many clusters simultaneously.
insights.quote: show upstream open reading frame editing as a strategy for modulating endogenous regulatory networks and accessing the fungal chemical repertoire

insights.claim: The authors position filamentous fungi as both major ecological contributors and industrial hosts with vast biosynthetic capacity, framing the tool's relevance beyond pure academic interest into industrial biomanufacturing.
insights.speaker: Sun, Nie, Straub, Keum, Shen & Gao
insights.so_what: Industrial fungal hosts (e.g., Aspergillus species used for citric acid, enzymes) are a large existing manufacturing base; a precision-editing platform for them has an immediate installed-base market rather than needing new host development.
insights.quote: filamentous fungi, which are major ecological contributors and industrial hosts with vast biosynthetic capacity

insights.claim: The research was funded by an NIH grant (R35GM138207) to Xue Gao and University of Pennsylvania startup funds to Xue Gao and Yihui Shen, indicating a US federally-backed academic origin rather than industry-sponsored R&D.
insights.speaker: Sun, Nie, Straub, Keum, Shen & Gao
insights.so_what: Academic, federally-funded origin means the core IP path likely runs through university tech transfer, making this a candidate for licensing or spin-out rather than an already-commercialized product.
insights.quote: This work was supported by National Institutes of Health (NIH) grant (R35GM138207 to X.G.), the startup fund provided by the University of Pennsylvania to X.G. and Y.S.

insights.claim: The authors declare no competing interests, distinguishing this academic prime-editing tool paper from adjacent Nature Biotechnology content in the same issue where a commentator disclosed founder equity in gene-editing companies.
insights.speaker: Sun, Nie, Straub, Keum, Shen & Gao
insights.so_what: No disclosed commercial stake suggests the technology is not yet tied to a specific startup, leaving the licensing/spin-out question open for investors tracking the University of Pennsylvania's biotech IP pipeline.
insights.quote: The authors declare no competing interests.
